perturbation.original_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Buyer ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Buyer” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.changed_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Client ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Client” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Buyer' to 'Client' in the definition section, while the rest of the document refers to 'Buyer,' creates a contradiction. The agreement defines 'Client' but then imposes obligations on the 'Buyer'. This makes enforcement uncertain because it's unclear if obligations intended for the 'Buyer' also apply to the 'Client'. This contradicts in the entire document when it refers to 'Buyer' obligations but the definition changed to 'Client'
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of Broker providing Confidential Information to Buyer, Buyer agrees to the following: KEEP INFORMATION CONFIDENTIAL Buyer acknowledges that any Confidential Information disclosed to others may be damaging to the Business and the Seller. Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public. Buyer agree s not to disclos e Confidential Information to anyone other than its/his/her advisors and affiliates who both (a) have a need to know the information in connection with the Transaction ; and (b) have agreed by signing a copy of this agreement to be bound by the terms of this agreement . Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction . Buyer shall be legally responsible for the actions and omissions of Buyer’s advisors and affiliates.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Buyer' and the contradicted text imposes obligations on 'Buyer'. The changed text redefines 'Buyer' as 'Client', but the contradicted text continues to refer to and impose obligations on 'Buyer'. This creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the obligations intended for the 'Buyer' also apply to the newly defined 'Client'. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer acknowledges that any Confidential Information disclosed to others may be damaging to the Business and the Seller. Buyer understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public.
perturbation.changed_text: Applicant acknowledges that any Confidential Information disclosed to others may be damaging to the Business and the Seller. Applicant understands that Confidential Information includes, without limitation: the fact that Business is for sale; financial details; identity of suppliers and customers; and any information not generally known by public.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Buyer' with 'Applicant' without defining 'Applicant' creates ambiguity. The agreement now refers to 'Applicant' acknowledging confidentiality, but the defined term remains 'Buyer'. This introduces uncertainty as to who is bound by the confidentiality obligations. This contradicts in the previous definition of buyer/client.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Client ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Client” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Buyer,' which is consistent with the 'Client' and 'Buyer' terminology used in the contradicted text. The changed text replaces 'Buyer' with 'Applicant' without defining 'Applicant'. This misalignment of terminology introduces ambiguity regarding who is bound by the confidentiality obligations. The contradicted text defines 'Client' to include the potential buyer, thus creating a direct conflict between the original agreement specifying obligations for 'Buyer' (defined as Client), and the changed agreement now mentioning 'Applicant' who is not defined as the client/buyer in the contradicted text. This fits the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Buyer' to 'Receiving Party', without defining 'Receiving Party', makes it uncertain who must return the confidential information if the Transaction is not pursued. The Receiving Party obligation does not correspond to the definition of the 'Client' creating a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Client ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Client” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'Buyer' is responsible for returning Confidential Information. The changed text replaces 'Buyer' with 'Receiving Party'. The 'Contradicted Text' defines 'Client' which includes 'Buyer' and 'Buyer's broker'. Because the 'Receiving Party' is undefined, it's unclear if this refers to the 'Client' as defined in the 'Contradicted Text' or some other party, creating an inconsistency and therefore a contradiction according to the definition of Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: DIRECT ALL CONTACT THROUGH BROKER Buyer shall not contact the Seller or any other individual or entity associated with Seller or the Business including, without limitation , landlords, employees, suppliers or customers except upon the prior written consent of Seller .
perturbation.changed_text: DIRECT ALL CONTACT THROUGH BROKER Potential Acquirer shall not contact the Seller or any other individual or entity associated with Seller or the Business including, without limitation , landlords, employees, suppliers or customers except upon the prior written consent of Seller .
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Buyer' with 'Potential Acquirer', without a definition, creates confusion regarding who must direct all contact through the broker. The defined 'Client' may not be the 'Potential Acquirer,' making this obligation ambiguous and unenforceable. Now the reader, may thing they are different entities.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Client ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Client” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.justification: The original text used 'Buyer', which is implicitly understood in the context. The changed text uses 'Potential Acquirer' without defining it, while the contradicted text defines 'Client' to include 'potential buyer'. Replacing 'Buyer' with 'Potential Acquirer' creates ambiguity because the undefined 'Potential Acquirer' may or may not be the defined 'Client'. This means the obligation to direct contact through the broker becomes uncertain, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between the Interested Party (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing the term 'Interested Party' in the context of litigation costs introduces further ambiguity. It's unclear who 'Interested Party' is and whether they are distinct from the defined 'Client' or 'Buyer'. This inconsistency can lead to disputes over liability for costs in legal actions. The usage of the Interested party is un-defined and the 'Client' and 'Buyer' are.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Client ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Client” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies litigation between 'Buyer' and 'Seller', or 'Buyer' and 'Broker'. The changed text replaces 'Buyer' with 'Interested Party' in the first instance, but retains 'Buyer' in the second. The explanation states that 'Interested Party' is undefined, creating ambiguity. The contradicted text defines 'Client' as including the potential buyer and the buyer's broker. Introducing the undefined 'Interested Party' creates a contradiction because it is unclear if 'Interested Party' refers to the 'Client', the 'Buyer' or something else, especially regarding who is liable for costs in legal actions. This misaligned terminology, as described in the Perturbation Definitions, creates a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: We, the undersigned, understand and agree that this agreement is legally binding upon us. We understand that Seller and/or Broker have the right to seek any and all lawful remedies to enforce the terms of this agreement. We acknowledge that we have read an d understand the disclosures contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: We, the undersigned, understand and agree that this agreement is legally binding upon the Undisclosed Party. We understand that Seller and/or Broker have the right to seek any and all lawful remedies to enforce the terms of this agreement. We acknowledge that we have read an d understand the disclosures contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'us' to 'Undisclosed Party' without defining it creates a significant contradiction. The agreement is purportedly binding on the 'Undisclosed Party,' but the agreement never defines that term. It is still defining, 'Client', 'Buyer', leaving the agreement unenforceable against that 'Undisclosed Party.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: The u ndersigned (hereinafter , collectively, “Client ”) acknowledges its/his/her desire to receive from Epsteen & Associates (“ Broker ”) and from the owner of the Business, described above (“Seller”) certain information pertaining to the Business , the Seller and/or the possible sale of the Business (the “Transaction”). For purpose of this Agreement, (a) the term “Client” means all of undersigned, including both the potential buyer interested in the Transaction, and such buyer’s broker , and both such b uyer and buyer’s broker are bound by the provisions of this agreement; and (b) any information provided to Buyer, or otherwise learned by Buyer, concerning the Business, Seller or Transaction shall collectively be referred to herein as “Confidential Inform ation”.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'us' to 'Undisclosed Party' creates a strong contradiction. The original text had the agreement binding on 'us,' which is reasonably understood as the undersigned parties. The changed text makes the agreement binding on the 'Undisclosed Party,' which is never defined within the contract. The 'Contradicted Text' defines Client, Seller, and Broker. The 'Undisclosed Party' definition is not provided, making the agreement unenforceable against said party, contradicting the intent of the agreement outlined in the original text and creating a significant ambiguity.
